topics.teaser: England, France, and the Dutch — three reasons to cross the Atlantic.

narrative.label: Three Kingdoms
narrative.spoken: What did each country want from the New World? England wanted room and control — patents, plantations, and towns where dissenting Protestants could farm and worship after the Reformation cracked the old faith. France looked west while Henry VIII broke with Rome: Cartier climbed the St. Lawrence in fifteen thirty-four, heard kanata, and claimed a colder doorway than Spain's gold coast. The Dutch followed money, not monarchs — Henry Hudson probed the river that would carry his name in sixteen oh-nine, seasonal posts trading metal for beaver while English and French settlers built colonies on every side. Three appetites, one cold ocean, and four centuries before they meet in Iowa.
paragraphs: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with doors — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river no Englishman yet called home. By the fifteen seventies Elizabeth granted patents to men willing to risk North Atlantic fog while France was already building on the St. Lawrence.

England wanted room and control. After Luther's slow fire and Henry VIII's break with Rome, restless Puritans and yeomen wanted land where they could farm, worship, and govern. France wanted empire on a frozen river — furs, fish, and Catholic farming families to hold Québec. The Dutch wanted trade, not territory: beaver pelts and posts on the Hudson, Connecticut, and Delaware while others planted towns around them.

Two colonial stories had begun — separate, unaware that centuries later they would meet in an Iowa wedding. The Atlantic in those years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares.

topics.teaser: Edith Powers from England; Mary Ann Goodwater from Québec — one tree, two doorways.

narrative.label: Two Branches
narrative.spoken: Winifred's family tree is organized as two great branches. The Edith Powers branch begins in Cambridge in fifteen eighty-one with George Alcock and runs through Puritan New England to Warren Coss's marriage to Edith Powers. The Mary Ann Goodwater branch begins in Paris in fifteen seventy-five with Louis Hébert and the Bonneau line on the St. Lawrence. In eighteen fifty-three, Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater in Winneshiek County, Iowa — and the two branches become one.
paragraphs: In fifteen seventy-five the Goodwater doorway opened with Louis Hébert in Paris — not yet Canada's first farmer, simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells. Six years later, on twenty-five March fifteen eighty-one, George Alcock was born at Cambridge, son of a Protestant minister. No crown noted the day. But the Powers branch starts here, in a university town smelling of river mud and ink.

France was filling the St. Lawrence with names our cousins would carry — Couillard, Cloutier, Rollet, Giffard. England was waking to America in the same years: Gilbert, Raleigh, Winthrop's parish world, yeomen who preferred risk to stagnation.

The first branch formally ends when Warren Coss marries Edith Powers. The second ends when Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater on two April eighteen fifty-three. Everything before those doorways is prologue; everything after is the long walk toward Winifred.

topics.teaser: George Alcock in Cambridge, 1581 — the English doorway to Massachusetts Bay.

narrative.label: Powers Branch
narrative.spoken: The Powers doorway opens in Cambridge in fifteen eighty-one when George Alcock is born — son of a Protestant minister. Barrett, Powers, Preston, Rice, Upham, French, Towne, and Bent accumulate in English parish registers while the kingdom tears at its own faith. Thomas Barrett is born in Gloucestershire in sixteen oh-nine; his granddaughter will sit in a Boston jail. Walter Powers Sr. is baptized at Stanton St. Quinton in sixteen oh-five. William Preston, Edmund Rice, John Upham, and Thomas French Sr. are all in the font before anyone sails. By sixteen thirty the Winthrop Fleet carries that stock to Massachusetts Bay.
paragraphs: On twenty-five March fifteen eighty-one, George Alcock was born at Cambridge — the English line that would meet the Hébert line in Iowa. In Assington, Suffolk, John Winthrop and Thomas French Sr. shared a parish world with yeomen who could read Latin and track a plow.

The branch births in England read like rain on parish registers: William Preston in Chesham fifteen ninety-one; Edmund Rice in Berkhamsted fifteen ninety-four; John Upham in Devon; John Bent in Hampshire; William Towne in Norfolk; Thomas Barrett at Thornbury six January sixteen oh-nine; Walter Powers Sr. baptized Stanton St. Quinton eight July sixteen oh-five — the name Edith would carry to Vermont and Iowa.

When James I died in sixteen twenty-five, Charles inherited a kingdom short of money and long on pride. The Atlantic crossings laid the keel: Barrett, Powers, Preston, Rice, Upham, and French still in England but not for long.

topics.teaser: Hébert, Bonneau, and a French name that became Goodwater.

narrative.label: Goodwater Branch
narrative.spoken: The Goodwater branch opens with Louis Hébert in Paris in fifteen seventy-five and Jean Bonneau reaching Canada in sixteen fifty-three. Parish registers, Jesuit censuses, and Cyprien Tanguay's genealogical dictionary — the backbone Dad used to document every French line from Québec to Goodwater — tie Bonneau, Cloutier, and Guyon to Winifred. Joseph Bonneau farms Île d'Orléans; Benjamin the third translates Bonneau to Goodwater on a Vermont census. Mary Ann carries the name into Iowa.
paragraphs: In fifteen seventy-five Louis Hébert was born in Paris — the St. Lawrence doorway. Champlain's Québec and Hébert's hoe made the settlement real while parish ink recorded Couillard, Cloutier, and Giffard in Perche towns emptying into the river.

Jean Bonneau the Huguenot arrived sixteen fifty-three; Pierre joined him on Île d'Orléans. Joseph Bonneau married fille du roi Marie-Anne Lelong in sixteen seventy. Dad reconstructed these lines through Cyprien Tanguay's Dictionnaire généalogique — the nineteenth-century parish digest that lets French cousins prove marriages, dit names, and migrations the English side tracks in county courthouses.

Bonneau means good water. Benjamin Bonneau III took that English word on the census; Mary Ann, born Lancaster eighteen thirty-nine, married Joseph Warren Coss in Iowa eighteen fifty-three — Tanguay's dictionary on one branch, Puritan registers on the other, one tree at last.

topics.teaser: Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater — the two branches join in Iowa.

narrative.label: Where the Branches Meet
narrative.spoken: On two April eighteen fifty-three, twenty-seven-year-old Joseph Warren Coss married fifteen-year-old Mary Ann Goodwater in Winneshiek County, Iowa. The Powers line through Edith and the Goodwater line through Mary Louise Carrière — Chambly stone, Red River canoes, Vermont border crossings, Lancaster birth records — met in a courthouse on the edge of corn. Puritan New England and French Canada become one Coss family twenty miles south of Minnesota. That wedding is the hinge of Winifred's story.
paragraphs: Joseph Warren Coss was born in eighteen twenty-seven to Warren Coss and Edith Powers — the Powers branch already folded into the Coss name. Mary Ann Goodwater was born Mary Ann Bonneau in Lancaster, New York, in eighteen thirty-nine; her father had anglicized the family name and moved from Vermont to Winneshiek County by eighteen fifty.

Eighteen fifty was the year ownership became visible. Warren and Edith farmed at Palmyra, Wisconsin, with six children — Joseph already gone west. Benjamin Goodwater III farmed in Ossian, Winneshiek County, thirteen hundred miles from Vermont by canal, lake, and rail.

Two adventures that began in Cambridge and Paris four centuries apart finally shook hands in an Iowa courthouse. The wedding was not an ending — it was a departure toward Dakota wheat, a Grand Forks hotel, and a girl named Winifred.

topics.teaser: Henry VIII, Luther, and the faith wars that pushed people toward the sea.

narrative.label: Reformation & Doorways
narrative.spoken: The story begins with doors, not people. In fourteen eighty-five the last Plantagenet king fell at Bosworth and a Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry the Eighth broke with Rome; Luther hammered his theses in Wittenberg in fifteen seventeen. Cartier claimed the St. Lawrence for France in fifteen thirty-four. Edward's English prayer book, Mary's fires at Smithfield, Elizabeth's compromise worn like armor — each turn sent more English minds toward a New World. By fifteen ninety, James the Sixth was hunting witches in Scotland — a panic this family will meet again in Massachusetts.
paragraphs: In fourteen eighty-five, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith.

Across the Channel in fifteen seventeen, Martin Luther hammered his objections onto a church door in Wittenberg. The Protestant Reformation was not a single explosion — it was a slow fire moving through northern Europe. Henry VIII dissolved the monasteries between fifteen thirty-six and fifteen forty-one; Edward's Book of Common Prayer made faith something you could be fined for; Mary's fires and Calvin's Geneva writings sharpened Puritan minds.

While England wrestled with conscience, France looked west. Elizabeth's answer was compromise worn like armor — patents to men willing to risk ships on North Atlantic fog. England was learning to look past its own shoreline. France was already building on the St. Lawrence. Two colonial stories had begun.

topics.teaser: 1630 — two thousand souls, eleven ships, Massachusetts Bay from Boston mud.

narrative.label: Winthrop Fleet
narrative.spoken: April sixteen thirty changed the English branch forever. John Winthrop led Puritans toward a patent from the Merrimack to the Charles — eleven ships, roughly two thousand souls. Salem could not feed them; Boston rose from Shawmut mud. George Alcock at Roxbury, Thomas French on the Arabella, Roger Williams on the Lyon — freemen oaths and town meetings planting Massachusetts Bay. Thomas Hooker drove congregants west to the Connecticut River; Roger Williams would seed Rhode Island. While Charles the First lost his head in sixteen forty-nine, cousins farmed Sudbury and Québec alike. Colonies spread toward New Hampshire, Connecticut, and the hills that would become Vermont.
paragraphs: The Massachusetts Bay Company charter arrived in sixteen twenty-nine without requiring meetings in England. Cambridge investors voted to move the whole company to America and elected Winthrop governor while he was still in Lincolnshire. April sixteen thirty: eleven ships, livestock, salted fear — George Alcock freeman at Roxbury, Thomas French on the Arabella, hundreds dying before the Lyon brought grain.

Sixteen thirty-five ships — Hopewell, James, Confidence, Truelove — carried Walter Powers Sr., Thomas Barrett, William Towne, and Phineas Upham. Harvard opened sixteen thirty-six; Thomas Hooker founded Hartford, Wethersfield, and Windsor on the Connecticut River with freeman rules looser than the Bay. Roger Williams on the Lyon pointed toward the colony that became Rhode Island.

While plantations grew, England convulsed. Charles I was beheaded thirty January sixteen forty-nine; Cromwell ruled while John Eliot organized Praying Indian villages and Scottish prisoners sailed as indentured servants. New Hampshire chartered separately in sixteen seventy-nine; towns pushed west to Leicester, Shrewsbury, Woodstock, and the Nashoba land that would become Littleton — spreading colonies long before Vermont grants and the walk to Iowa.

topics.teaser: Champlain, Hébert, and a Catholic colony on a frozen river.

narrative.label: New France
narrative.spoken: In fifteen thirty-four Cartier climbed the St. Lawrence toward Stadacona and heard kanata — France's doorway colder than Spain's gold coast. Champlain built Québec in sixteen oh-eight; Louis Hébert became the first European farmer behind the walls. Richelieu's Company of One Hundred Associates brought the seigneurial system — river-front manors three by nine miles, habitants owing mill rights and road work. Robert Giffard recruited from Perche with three-year contracts for land and livestock. Filles à marier sailed before the King's Daughters. Life meant frozen winters, Iroquois raids, Jesuit bells, and lime kilns — while beaver wars politicized the interior before our cousins cleared fields.
paragraphs: Québec settlement began with Cartier at Stadacona in fifteen thirty-four and Champlain's habitation in sixteen oh-eight. Hébert married Marie Rollet in Paris sixteen oh-one and by sixteen seventeen was farming wheat and rye — not counting pelts but holding a hoe. Upper town for elites, lower town for merchants: a French ville on a cliff above a river that froze six months a year.

Cardinal Richelieu's Company of One Hundred Associates in sixteen twenty-seven promised four thousand settlers and installed the seigneurial system — seigneurs subdividing river-front lots, habitants paying cens et rentes, feudal obligations dressed as settlement policy. Robert Giffard sailed home to Perche preaching contracts: three years' labor for a farm of your own. In March sixteen thirty-four he brought thirty Perche families — Guyon the mason, Cloutier the carpenter, Drouin the brickmaker — signing servitude for eight hundred forty-five acres.

Life in the Canada colony meant sour milk winters, plague ships, earthquakes scouring the St. Lawrence, and registers filling with filles à marier — two hundred sixty-two marriageable women in twenty-eight years before the filles du roi program began. Iroquois with Dutch firearms raided Trois-Rivières and Ville-Marie; the beaver wars emptied Huron villages while Champlain fought beside Wendat allies at Sorel. This cold doorway is where the Goodwater branch learns church, farm, and fear in the same breath.

topics.teaser: Filles du roi, founding families, and the island in the middle of the river.

narrative.label: King's Daughters
narrative.spoken: France needed families, not fur traders alone. Filles à marier sailed first — two hundred sixty-two women in twenty-eight years. Then the filles du roi with parish certificates and crown dowries. The Carignan-Salières Regiment landed in sixteen sixty-five — twelve hundred soldiers, forts on the Richelieu, Tracy's campaign against Mohawk villages. The Grande Rescue of sixteen fifty-three brought Jean Bonneau and a hundred recruits to save Ville-Marie. Joseph Bonneau farmed Île d'Orléans and married Marie-Anne Lelong in sixteen seventy. Dad's French lines trace through Cyprien Tanguay's dictionary — the genealogical backbone for every Bonneau, Cloutier, and Guyon in this tree.
paragraphs: Before the filles du roi came the filles à marier — marriageable girls sent to populate the St. Lawrence, average ten per year, Marie Crevet and Françoise Morin among them. The filles à marier program ended in sixteen sixty-three when Louis XIV made Canada a royal province; the King's Daughters followed with priest recommendations and merchant-paid dowries — seven hundred sixty-eight recruited, twenty-four tying directly to Mary Ann Goodwater's branch.

The Carignan-Salières Regiment arrived June through September sixteen sixty-five — hand-picked soldiers fresh from fighting Turks, delayed at La Rochelle for canoe-building. Forts rose on the Richelieu; Tracy destroyed Mohawk villages in sixteen sixty-six. Discharged soldiers stayed; Jean Besset married a fille du roi at Fort Chambly. The Grande Rescue of sixteen fifty-three landed Jean Bonneau the Huguenot and Catherine Hurault at Montréal when Ville-Marie had shrunk below fifty souls.

Joseph Bonneau finished indenture on Île d'Orléans — one of three hundred founding families on the long island — and married Marie-Anne Lelong at Sainte-Famille in sixteen seventy. Parish registers, Jesuit censuses, and later Cyprien Tanguay's Dictionnaire généalogique — published from eighteen sixty-five — are the genealogical backbone Dad used to prove every French cousin from Hébert to Goodwater. Without Tanguay's pages, the Bonneau line is names without witnesses.

topics.teaser: Pequot, King Philip's, and the Beaver Wars — frontier fire on both rivers.

narrative.label: Colonial Wars
narrative.spoken: Sergeant Thomas French Jr. fought the Pequot War — five hundred dead in the Mystic fort fire of sixteen thirty-seven. John Eliot's Praying Indian villages, including Nashoba granted to Chief Tahattawan in sixteen fifty-one, wore English coats and Puritan meetinghouses on land that would become Littleton. King Philip's War destroyed twelve towns; Nashoba people starved on Deer Island; Phineas Upham died after the Great Swamp Fight. In Canada, Iroquois beaver wars emptied Huron country; Ville-Marie shrank under Mohawk attack until the Grande Rescue. The family is in militia rolls, garrison houses, and prison lists — never merely nearby.
paragraphs: Colonial–Native wars shaped the English thread from the start. Pequot War sixteen thirty-six to sixteen thirty-eight: Sergeant Thomas French Jr. on the ledger that includes the Mystic Massacre of twenty-six May sixteen thirty-seven. John Eliot and Daniel Gookin built fourteen Praying Indian towns — Bibles in Algonquin, English houses — including Nashoba for Chief Tahattawan's Pennacook people, land later renamed Littleton.

Sixteen seventy-five: King Philip's War — twelve towns destroyed, one in ten military-age men dead. The Great Swamp Fight in Rhode Island; Groton burned; Lt. Phineas Upham died of wounds; Martha Barrett Sparks's husband Henry Sparks fought west while she sat in Boston jail. Nashoba Praying Indians rounded up to Deer Island starved; Robert Robbins would buy their confiscated acres in sixteen eighty-six.

On the St. Lawrence, beaver wars armed Iroquois with Dutch firearms against Ville-Marie and Trois-Rivières. Canada counted three hundred fifty souls against twelve thousand Haudenosaunee. The Grande Rescue and Carignan steel answered trade wars the English fought as Pequot and King Philip. Both branches lived inside the fire, not beside it.

topics.teaser: Martha Barrett Sparks in a Boston jail — a direct Powers-branch tie.

narrative.label: Salem Witch Panic
narrative.spoken: James the Sixth published Daemonologie in fifteen ninety-seven — witchcraft as a legal idea that would cross the border when he became James the First. Before sixteen ninety-two, accusation was costly because prisoners paid jail fees and could sue for slander. Spring sixteen ninety-two changed everything: spectral evidence, guilt assumed, shackles and starvation. Martha Barrett Sparks of Chelmsford was imprisoned twenty-eight October sixteen ninety-one while Henry Sparks fought away from home. Her father petitioned Governor Phips; she walked free eight December after fifty-eight weeks — the Barrett line into Powers, not a footnote near Salem.
paragraphs: Witchcraft was a capital crime long before Salem village erupted. Margaret Jones the Charlestown midwife was hanged in sixteen forty-eight — first of fifteen New England executions before sixteen ninety-three. James VI's Daemonologie in fifteen ninety-seven framed witches as a crown concern; when he united England and Scotland in sixteen oh-three, the idea traveled with his Bible.

Spring sixteen ninety-two rewrote the law while Governor Phips fought Natives in the west. Courts accepted spectral evidence — a witness could swear the accused's spirit attacked in a dream. Accused witches paid their own jail costs; conviction meant forfeited property whether innocent or guilty.

At Chelmsford, Martha Barrett Sparks — granddaughter of Thomas Barrett — was imprisoned twenty-eight October sixteen ninety-one, fifty-eight weeks beside Salem women, never tried. Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned one November sixteen ninety-two; Martha released eight December. Rebecca Barrett married Walter Powers III in sixteen ninety-six; Josiah Powers Sr. was born on Neshoba Hill in seventeen ten — the Salem witch trials as family law, not history-class scenery.

topics.teaser: King William's through the French & Indian War — Québec becomes British.

narrative.label: British North America
narrative.spoken: Colonies spread — Rhode Island, Connecticut, New Hampshire, Leicester, Shrewsbury, Littleton on stolen Praying land, then Vermont grants and Neshoba renamed Brandon. King William's through the French and Indian War rearranged the map; Treaty of Paris seventeen sixty-three made New France British while French civil law and Catholic worship continued under Governor Murray. Josiah Powers Jr. enlisted in seventeen seventy-five, fought Bunker Hill, helped write Vermont's constitution, and marched with Stark's Green Mountain Boys at Bennington. Benjamin Bonneau III was born on the Richelieu in eighteen eleven. Warren Coss waited in Lowell for Edith Powers of Athens, Vermont.
paragraphs: Spreading colonies carried the English thread west and north. Thomas Hooker opened Connecticut; Roger Williams seeded Rhode Island; New Hampshire chartered sixteen seventy-nine; Eliot's Nashoba became Littleton; Joshua Lamb's investors founded Woodstock. Capt. Josiah Powers Sr. was born in a Littleton garrison house in seventeen ten; his father Walter III kept an inn near Spectacle Pond on land Praying Indians once farmed.

British Québec began when Montcalm and Wolfe died on the Plains of Abraham in seventeen fifty-nine and Treaty of Paris seventeen sixty-three gave Britain all of New France. Governor Murray kept French civil law, Catholic parishes, and seigneurial custom — farmers stayed on Richelieu riverlots under a Protestant king. King George's Royal Proclamation seventeen sixty-three drew a line at the Appalachians that infuriated Yankee speculators.

Revolution and west followed. Josiah Powers Jr. enlisted Reed's Third New Hampshire, fought seventeen June seventeen seventy-five at Bunker Hill, helped write Vermont's seventeen seventy-seven constitution, and fought Bennington with Green Mountain Boys. Benjamin Bonneau III born Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu eighteen eleven; Warren Coss born Lowell seventeen ninety-one; Edith Powers born Athens, Vermont, eighteen hundred — revolution, conquest, and grants pointing both branches toward Iowa.

topics.teaser: Vermont to Wisconsin to Winneshiek — the long walk before the wedding.

narrative.label: West to Iowa
narrative.spoken: After the Revolution, the English branch learned Vermont hill towns and Erie Canal timetables — Warren Coss married Edith Powers at Lowell in eighteen twenty-four; Nathaniel and Esther Powers at Westminster and Athens. Joseph Warren Coss was born eighteen twenty-seven. Benjamin Bonneau III married métis Mary Louise Carrière, crossed to Lancaster, New York, and anglicized to Goodwater. The Black Hawk Purchase opened Iowa in eighteen thirty-three. Joseph walked west to Winneshiek County; on two April eighteen fifty-three he married Mary Ann — Continental soldiers' grandchildren and Richelieu masons' descendants in one Iowa courthouse.
paragraphs: Revolution's westward push continued long after Yorktown. Josiah Powers Jr. farmed Marlboro and Chesterfield, Vermont; Nathaniel married Esther Johnson at Westminster eighteen hundred; Edith Powers born Athens that October — eight miles from Westminster, twenty-five from grandfather Josiah's farm.

Thirty September eighteen twenty-four, Warren Coss married Edith at Lowell — the Coss stub grafted onto the Powers limb. Benjamin Bonneau III married Mary Louise Carrière in eighteen thirty-one, slipped fifty miles south across the border, and settled Lancaster before anglicizing Bonneau to Goodwater. Warren and Edith rode the canal to Wisconsin after eighteen forty-one; Joseph left for Winneshiek before eighteen fifty.

Two April eighteen fifty-three: Joseph Warren Coss married fifteen-year-old Mary Ann Goodwater in Winneshiek County — Vermont grants, Red River métis blood, Chambly stone, and Massachusetts militia habits converging on Iowa corn. The Revolution and west thread ends where the Dakota years begin.

topics.teaser: Grand Forks hotel, frontier towns, and Mary Ann's death in 1878.

narrative.label: Dakota Years
narrative.spoken: After the Iowa wedding, Joseph and Mary Ann pushed through Wisconsin and Minnesota — Cherry Grove, Getty, Little Sauk. In eighteen seventy-six they traveled two hundred thirty miles north to Grand Forks in Dakota Territory and opened the first hotel — tepees still nearby, bison on the horizon. Mary Ann died there in eighteen seventy-eight at thirty-nine. Edgar Putnam Coss married Sarah Collingwood and raised sons through bank panics. Charles Whitcomb Coss was born in eighteen ninety; Winifred Eloise followed in nineteen twenty-two while her father worked Grand Forks fire.
paragraphs: Eight November eighteen fifty-five, Edgar Putnam Coss was born at Sauk Centre, Wisconsin. Joseph enlisted in the First Minnesota Heavy Artillery in eighteen sixty-five and guarded Chattanooga's rail junction while Sherman marched. Warren Coss died at Concord, Wisconsin, in eighteen sixty-nine.

Eighteen seventy-six: Custer fell on the Little Bighorn while Joseph and Mary Ann moved north to Grand Forks and opened the first hotel. Twenty-three September eighteen seventy-eight, Mary Ann Goodwater died at thirty-nine. Fourteen April eighteen eighty, Edgar married seventeen-year-old Sarah Collingwood at Grand Forks.

Joseph Warren Coss died of apoplexy in nineteen oh-eight under a Union soldier's stone at Palmyra. Charles married Ethel Raymond in nineteen twenty; thirty August nineteen twenty-two, Winifred Eloise Coss was born at Arvilla while her father worked Grand Forks fire. Eighteen December nineteen twenty-seven, Ethel died — Winifred went to aunt Lorraine Geiger at Kenmare.

topics.teaser: Crowded households, roomers, and farms that would not yield.

narrative.label: Depression & Dust Bowl
narrative.spoken: Twenty-four October nineteen twenty-nine, Wall Street broke. North Dakota fell twice — wheat prices collapsed while drought turned the western half to powder. Nineteen thirty-four and nineteen thirty-six piled dirt like snowdrifts. Winifred was seven at Kenmare with aunt and uncle Geiger, already motherless. The nineteen thirty census found Edgar unemployed on Grand Forks' floodplain; Winfield Geiger owned a Kenmare service station. Firemen's wages and roomers' rent kept the family alive while farms failed.
paragraphs: Twenty-four October nineteen twenty-nine, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned prairie to powder. More than a hundred twenty-one thousand people left the state; twelve thousand farms were foreclosed.

Nineteen thirty census told the damage in rows. Edgar P. Coss, seventy-five, unemployed at thirty-one Euclid Avenue; widower Charles and grandsons crowded the house with firemen's wages. Winfield Geiger owned a Kenmare service station; seven-year-old niece Winifred lived there with roomers helping cover rent.

Eleven March nineteen thirty-two, William James Raymond died at Arvilla; two days later his wife Annie followed. Winifred grew up through it — nineteen forty census listed her at seventeen, a high school junior in Kenmare while uncles Clarence and Charles Jr. worked Grand Forks fire beside her father.

topics.teaser: The paper is written for her — Winifred Eloise Coss, 1922–2000s.

narrative.label: Winifred Eloise Coss
narrative.spoken: Winifred Eloise Coss is why the paper exists. Born to Charles Whitcomb Coss and Ethel Raymond in North Dakota in nineteen twenty-two; raised by the Geigers after her mother's death in nineteen twenty-seven; married Morris Rankin Batie in Las Vegas in nineteen forty-two. Four centuries of England and Québec, witch panic and world wars, filles du roi and Dust Bowl winds — all preserved so a grandchild could hear the names aloud. From George Alcock in Cambridge and Louis Hébert in Paris, the family tree that began as two doors opening ended as one woman with the name the whole book had been carrying.
paragraphs: The following paper is the story of Winifred Eloise Coss's family tree — not a dry chart, but two vast journeys that meet in Iowa and descend to her in North Dakota. Famous people open the chapters; your relatives walk through them.

Thirty August nineteen twenty-two, Winifred was born at Arvilla while her father worked Grand Forks fire. Eighteen December nineteen twenty-seven, Ethel died at twenty-eight; Charles gave five-year-old Winifred to sister Lorraine Geiger and husband Winfield at Kenmare. She grew up through Depression and dust while Coss men stayed on the Red River with hoses and horses.

Twenty-five April nineteen forty-two, twenty-year-old Winifred married Morris Rankin Batie in Las Vegas. Between nineteen twenty-nine and nineteen fifty the story finished its arc — a stock crash and a dust cloud took farms Homestead Acts had given, and one woman with a new family name carried four hundred years forward. Start again from the beginning, or open any chapter — the people are still here.
